mission.title: Install and Configure Kuasar on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kuasar is a multi-sandbox container runtime that provides cloud-native solutions for various container scenarios. Installing Kuasar allows you to leverage its efficient container management capabilities, enhancing your cloud-native applications.

steps.title: Add the Kuasar Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Kuasar Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kuasar https://kuasar.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kuasar using Helm
steps.description:
Install Kuasar in a new namespace called 'kuasar' with the following command:
```bash
helm install kuasar kuasar/kuasar --namespace kuasar --create-namespace --version v1.0.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Kuasar pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kuasar
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the Kuasar deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to open the deployment in your editor:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment kuasar --namespace kuasar
```
Then, add the following resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
```

steps.title: Expose Kuasar service
steps.description:
Expose the Kuasar service to access it externally using port-forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/kuasar --namespace kuasar 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kuasar will have the pods running without errors, and you will be able to access the service through port 8080 on your local machine. Additionally, resource limits will be configured for optimal performance.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kuasar https://kuasar.io/charts
helm repo update
helm install kuasar kuasar/kuasar --namespace kuasar --create-namespace --version v1.0.1

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kuasar Helm release from the cluster using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall kuasar --namespace kuasar
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources associated with Kuasar. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep kuasar | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the Kuasar namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kuasar
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of the Kuasar release. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
helm get values kuasar --namespace kuasar > kuasar-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Kuasar release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kuasar kuasar/kuasar --namespace kuasar --version v1.0.2
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the Kuasar pods to ensure they are running the upgraded version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kuasar
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kuasar pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kuasar
```Fix any configuration issues or resource limits that may be causing the crashes.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kuasar service is not reachable, ensure that the service is running and check for any network policies:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kuasar
kubectl describe svc kuasar --namespace kuasar
```Verify that the port-forwarding command is set correctly.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails due to a version conflict, check the installed version and the available versions:
```bash
helm list --namespace kuasar
helm search repo kuasar/kuasar --versions
```Ensure you are upgrading to a compatible version.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are not starting due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment kuasar --namespace kuasar
```Adjust the resource limits in the deployment if necessary.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have Kubernetes cluster access and have installed Helm and kubectl.
